original_abstract: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are of considerable concern due to their well-recognised toxicity and especially due to the carcinogenic hazard which they present. PAHs are semi-volatile and therefore partition between vapour and condensed phases in the atmosphere and both the vapour and particulate forms undergo chemical reactions. This article briefly reviews the current understanding of vapour-particle partitioning of PAHs and the PAH deposition processes, and in greater detail, their chemical reactions. PAHs are reactive towards a number of atmospheric oxidants, most notably the hydroxyl radical, ozone, the nitrate radical (NO3) and nitrogen dioxide. Rate coefficient data are reviewed for reactions of lower molecular weight PAH vapour with these species as well as for heterogeneous reactions of higher molecular weight compounds. Whereas the data for reactions of the 2-3-ring PAH vapour are quite extensive and generally consistent, such data are mostly lacking for the 4-ring PAHs and the heterogeneous rate data (5 and more rings), which are dependent on the substrate type and reaction conditions, are less comprehensive. The atmospheric reactions of PAH lead to the formation of oxy and nitro derivatives, reviewed here, too. Finally, the capacity of PAHs for long range transport and the results of numerical model studies are described. Research needs are identified.
humanized_abstract: Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) are highly toxic and are known carcinogens. Therefore their presence in the atmosphere is of considerable concern. The semi-volatility of PAHs results in the development of a complex atmospheric chemistry where these compounds are in a dynamic vapour/condensate equilibrium existing together with reactive species in all phases. The vapour/condensate partitioning, and PAH deposition are briefly discussed. In more detail, the atmospheric chemistry of PAHs which generally concerns their reactions with the hydroxyl radical, ozone, nitrate radical and nitrogen dioxide are described and documented with available rate coefficients for lower molecular weight PAHs in the vapour phase as well as for heterogeneous reactions of higher molecular weight PAHs. While the rate coefficients for reactions of lower molecular weight PAHs (2-3 rings) are generally available and consistent, they are however only limited for the 4-ring PAHs and are very limited for heterogeneous reactions for higher molecular weight PAHs (5 and more rings) which are highly dependent on the surface composition of the reactive substrate and on the conditions of the reactions. The atmospheric transformations of PAHs that form oxy- and nitro-PAHs are also reviewed. Finally, the long range transport capabilities of PAHs and the results of modelling studies are discussed. Needs for further research are highlighted.
